title: Multinational companies (MNCs)
summary: A multinational company does not just sell abroad - it plants factories, offices and stores on foreign soil, and in doing so reshapes the economies it enters. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 1.6: the characteristics that make a business an MNC; the reasons firms go multinational (access to markets, cheaper resources and labour, avoiding trade barriers and economies of scale); and the impact of MNCs on the host country, both positive (jobs, investment, technology transfer, tax revenue and consumer choice) and negative (pressure on local competition, profit repatriation, exploitation concerns and environmental and cultural effects). Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks - AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation - so you can see why a balanced answer ending in a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Define a multinational company (MNC) and identify the characteristics that distinguish it from an exporter or a purely domestic business.
Explain why businesses become multinational, using the drivers of market access, cheaper resources and labour, avoiding trade barriers and economies of scale.
Analyse the positive and negative impacts of an MNC on a host country and weigh them against each other.
Apply MNC concepts to an unseen case study and reach a supported judgement about the net impact on a host country.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: From local hero to global giant
simpleExplanation.summary: A multinational company (MNC) is a business that does not merely sell its products abroad but actually sets up operations - factories, offices, call centres or stores - in other countries. It does this to reach more customers, cut its costs and grow larger than its home market alone would allow. Wherever it lands (the host country), it brings a mix of benefits and drawbacks that a government has to weigh.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a popular local pizza place. Shipping frozen pizzas to France makes it an exporter, not a multinational. It becomes an MNC only when it takes the bigger step of opening a full restaurant in France - its own kitchen, its own staff, its own rent - investing directly on foreign soil. That single move brings jobs and choice to the French town, but it also puts pressure on the family-run trattoria next door, and most of the profit is wired back home. That trade-off, repeated on a giant scale, is the whole story of MNCs and their host countries.
steps: A business first builds a strong brand and profitable operations in its home country.
To keep growing, it looks abroad for new customers, cheaper resources or a more favourable business environment.
It makes a foreign direct investment (FDI) - setting up physical operations such as a factory or store - in a host country.

That presence creates benefits for the host (jobs, investment, technology) alongside costs (pressure on local firms, profit repatriation, possible exploitation), and the balance between them is what exam answers must weigh.

flashcards.back: A business that has its headquarters in one country (the home country) but owns and controls production or service operations in at least one other country (the host country). Also called a transnational corporation (TNC).
A nation that allows a multinational company based elsewhere to set up and run operations within its borders.
The country where a multinational company has its main headquarters and where profits are typically returned.

Investment by a business into physical operations or controlling ownership in another country. FDI is the defining action that turns a business into an MNC - it is what separates operating abroad from merely selling abroad.

An exporter produces goods in its home country and ships them abroad to sell. An MNC physically operates and produces (or delivers services) inside the foreign country itself. The difference is FDI, not sales.

Direct operations in more than one country; a parent company with foreign subsidiaries; large scale and significant economic power; and a global strategy made to benefit the whole corporation rather than any single host country.
Access to larger markets; cheaper resources and labour; avoiding trade barriers by producing inside a market or bloc; and economies of scale from operating on a global scale.
The cost advantages gained from producing on a large, global scale - such as bulk buying of raw materials and mass production - which lower the average cost per unit.
A government-imposed restriction on imports, such as a tariff (import tax) or quota (import limit). Producing inside the country or trading bloc lets an MNC avoid these barriers.
The introduction of new technology, skills and management techniques into a host country when an MNC trains local workers and brings in modern equipment and know-how.
When an MNC sends the profit earned in a host country back to its home country instead of reinvesting it locally - meaning the host economy retains less of the wealth the MNC generates.

An accounting practice where divisions of the same MNC set prices for goods and services traded between them. It can be used legitimately, but also controversially to shift reported profit into low-tax countries and reduce tax paid in the host country.

flashcards.front: Positive impacts on a host country
flashcards.back: Job creation, inward investment (FDI) that raises GDP, technology and skills transfer, tax revenue, better infrastructure and greater consumer choice.

flashcards.front: Negative impacts on a host country
flashcards.back: Local firms driven out by superior scale and pricing, profit repatriation, exploitation of low-wage labour, environmental damage, cultural erosion and reduced tax through avoidance.
Selling products abroad does NOT make a business an MNC - that is exporting. An MNC must own and run operations in another country. The dividing line is foreign direct investment.

The impact of an MNC on a host country is neither all good nor all bad. A top-band answer weighs jobs, investment and technology AGAINST repatriation, exploitation and pressure on local firms, then judges which outweighs for the specific context.

quickCheck.prompt: A UK toy-maker produces all its toys in Britain and ships them to shops in Germany, France and Spain, where they sell well. Is this business a multinational company?
options: Yes, because it earns revenue in several countries
Yes, because its products are sold across borders
No - it only exports; it becomes an MNC only if it sets up operations abroad (foreign direct investment)
No, because it is too small to be an MNC
quickCheck.answer: No - it only exports; it becomes an MNC only if it sets up operations abroad (foreign direct investment)

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following best explains why a business would build a factory INSIDE a trading bloc such as the EU rather than exporting to it?
options: To increase the wages it pays its workers
To avoid the tariffs (import taxes) that would apply to goods brought in from outside the bloc
To reduce the size of its total market
To guarantee it will never face any competition
quickCheck.answer: To avoid the tariffs (import taxes) that would apply to goods brought in from outside the bloc

quickCheck.prompt: An MNC earns large profits at its factory in a developing host country but sends most of that profit back to its home-country headquarters. What is this called, and why is it a concern for the host country?
options: Economies of scale - it lowers the host country's costs
Technology transfer - it upgrades the host country's skills
Profit repatriation - the wealth generated leaves the host economy instead of being reinvested there
Foreign direct investment - it increases the host country's capital stock
quickCheck.answer: Profit repatriation - the wealth generated leaves the host economy instead of being reinvested there

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a POSITIVE impact of an MNC on a host country, rather than a negative one?
options: Local firms are forced out of business by the MNC's economies of scale
The MNC transfers new technology and trains local workers in modern skills
Profits are shifted to low-tax countries through transfer pricing
The MNC ignores environmental standards to cut costs
quickCheck.answer: The MNC transfers new technology and trains local workers in modern skills

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'An MNC always benefits a host country because it creates jobs.' Why would an examiner mark this down?
options: Job creation is not actually a benefit of MNCs
It is one-sided - it ignores negatives such as profit repatriation, exploitation and pressure on local firms, and reaches no balanced, supported judgement
MNCs never create any jobs in host countries
The statement uses the wrong technical term for job creation
quickCheck.answer: It is one-sided - it ignores negatives such as profit repatriation, exploitation and pressure on local firms, and reaches no balanced, supported judgement

faq.q: What is the difference between an MNC and a business that just exports?
faq.a: An exporter makes its products at home and sells them abroad; nothing about its operations leaves the home country. An MNC goes further and makes a foreign direct investment - it physically sets up part of its business (a factory, office, store or call centre) inside another country. The test is not where the sales happen but where the operations are. A firm can export to fifty countries and still not be an MNC; a firm that owns a single overseas factory already is one.

faq.q: Why are MNCs often criticised over tax?
faq.a: Large MNCs can legally use complex structures and transfer pricing - setting the prices at which their own divisions trade with each other - to report most of their profit in countries with very low corporation tax, even when their sales and operations are elsewhere. The result is that they contribute less tax to the host countries where they actually generate revenue. It is often legal, but many regard it as unethical, and it is a standard negative point in host-country evaluations.

faq.q: What does 'profit repatriation' mean and why does it matter to a host country?
faq.a: Repatriation is when the MNC sends the profit it earns in a host country back to its home-country headquarters instead of reinvesting it locally. It matters because a host government hopes FDI will circulate wealth through its economy - wages spent locally, profits reinvested in expansion. If profits are largely repatriated, the host country keeps the jobs and wages but loses much of the profit, which weakens the long-term economic benefit of hosting the MNC.

faq.q: Can an MNC be a socially responsible business?
faq.a: Yes. While many MNCs are criticised, others pursue corporate social responsibility (CSR) - investing in local communities, adopting fair and sustainable practices, and ensuring decent pay and conditions. How 'responsible' an MNC is varies enormously from one company to another, so in an exam you should judge the specific business in the case study rather than assume all MNCs behave the same way.

faq.q: How do I answer a question on the impact of an MNC on a host country?
faq.a: Give both sides using the case study. Set the positives (jobs, investment, technology transfer, tax revenue, choice) against the negatives (pressure on local firms, profit repatriation, exploitation, environmental and cultural effects), applying each to the specific business and country in the stimulus. Then commit to a supported judgement - say whether the net impact is positive or negative here and why one side outweighs the other. The marks are in the application and the judgement, not in the length of the list.

sections.content: You are almost certainly dealing with multinational companies every day - the phone in your pocket, the trainers on your feet, the coffee in your hand and the apps you scroll are likely provided by them. These global giants are among the most powerful economic forces on the planet: the biggest earn more revenue than many countries produce in a year. But how do they grow so large, and when one of them opens operations in a developing country, is its arrival a blessing or a burden? This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 1.6 - what makes a business an MNC, why firms go multinational, and the two-sided impact they have on the countries that host them. Because 1.6 is an evaluation-heavy topic, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a balanced, applied judgement outscores a memorised list every time.

A multinational company (MNC), also called a transnational corporation (TNC), is a business that owns and controls production or service operations in at least one country other than its home country. The word that matters most is 'operations'. An MNC is not simply a business that sells its products internationally - that is an exporter. The defining feature is foreign direct investment (FDI): the MNC physically establishes part of its business on foreign soil, whether a factory, a call centre, a research lab or a chain of stores. A business can export to dozens of markets and still not be an MNC; the moment it owns and runs operations abroad, it is one.

items: **Operations in more than one country:** the MNC has a physical presence and manages activities in the host country, not just sales there. This is the single characteristic that defines an MNC.
**Parent and subsidiaries:** MNCs typically have a parent company in the home country controlling multiple subsidiaries in various host countries.
**Large scale and economic power:** their revenues can exceed the GDP of smaller nations, giving them real bargaining power with governments over tax, regulation and incentives.

**A global strategy:** major decisions are taken to benefit the whole corporation, which is not necessarily the same as benefiting any individual host country - the root of much of the controversy around MNCs.

sections.content: Going multinational is a major strategic decision driven by a mix of 'pull' factors (opportunities abroad) and 'push' factors (limits or pressures at home). The syllabus focuses on four core reasons, and a strong answer names the reason precisely rather than gesturing at 'growth' in general.

items: **Access to markets:** setting up in another country puts the business directly among millions of new customers, driving sales growth beyond what a saturated or small home market allows. Local operations also help the firm tailor products to local tastes.
**Cheaper resources and labour:** producing where wages, land or raw materials are cheaper - or where regulation is lighter - can cut production costs sharply and raise competitiveness.

**Avoiding trade barriers:** by producing inside a country or a trading bloc (such as the EU), an MNC sidesteps the tariffs and quotas that would apply to goods exported in from outside. Manufacturing locally turns an import into a domestically made product.

**Economies of scale:** operating globally lets the MNC buy raw materials in bulk, run mass-production facilities and spread fixed costs such as research and branding over far more units, lowering the average cost per unit.
**Spreading risk (supporting reason):** operating across several economies reduces dependence on any single market, so a downturn in one country can be offset by growth in another.

sections.question: A UK-based clothing company, BritWear, is considering moving production of 100,000 T-shirts per year to Vietnam. In the UK the labour cost per T-shirt is £3.50; in Vietnam it would be the equivalent of £0.70 per T-shirt. Moving would add £40,000 per year in shipping and administration costs. Calculate the total annual cost saving if BritWear moves production to Vietnam. [4]
sections.solution:
This question rewards accurate calculation set out step by step.

**Step 1: Total annual labour cost in the UK.**
£3.50 × 100,000 = £350,000

**Step 2: Total annual labour cost in Vietnam.**
£0.70 × 100,000 = £70,000

**Step 3: Total cost of the Vietnam option, including the extra shipping and admin.**
£70,000 + £40,000 = £110,000

**Step 4: Total annual cost saving.**
£350,000 − £110,000 = £240,000

**Answer: BritWear would save £240,000 per year.**

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:** the engine credits the method as well as the final figure, so a labelled working earns marks even if one arithmetic slip occurs (own-figure rule).

- **AO2 (1):** correct UK total (£350,000).
- **AO2 (1):** correct Vietnam labour total (£70,000).
- **AO2 (1):** correctly adds the £40,000 extra costs to reach £110,000.
- **AO4 (1):** states the final saving of £240,000 with the correct unit (£).

The cheaper-labour saving here is exactly the 'cheaper resources and labour' motive in action - but note the calculation only captures cost, not the risks (quality, lead time, reputation) that a full evaluation question would demand.

sections.content: The impact of MNCs on the host country

When an MNC arrives, it acts on the host country like a double-edged sword - bringing real opportunities and real threats at the same time. For the exam you must be able to argue both sides in a balanced way and, crucially, apply each point to the specific business and country in the stimulus. The lists below are the raw material; the marks come from weighing them against each other for a particular context.

items: **Positive impacts on the host country:**
**Jobs:** MNCs often employ large numbers of local people, reducing unemployment and raising household incomes, which are then spent locally.
**Investment:** the FDI, wages and spending an MNC brings inject money into the economy and raise the host country's GDP.
**Technology transfer:** local workers gain new skills and management techniques, and modern technology and equipment enter the country, raising long-term productivity.
**Tax revenue:** profits, wages and sales generate tax the government can spend on public services and infrastructure.
**Consumer choice:** MNCs introduce new products and services, often at competitive prices, widening the options available to local consumers.
**Infrastructure:** MNCs may fund roads, ports and communications to support their operations, which local firms and residents can also use.
**Negative impacts on the host country:**
**Pressure on local competition:** small local firms may be unable to match the MNC's economies of scale, prices and marketing, and can be driven out of business.
**Profit repatriation:** much of the profit may be sent back to the home country rather than reinvested locally, so the host keeps the jobs but loses the wealth.
**Exploitation concerns:** to cut costs, some MNCs are accused of paying very low wages, demanding long hours or tolerating unsafe conditions, especially in developing countries.
**Environmental effects:** cost-cutting can mean pollution, resource depletion or ignoring environmental standards that a host government struggles to enforce.
**Cultural effects:** the dominance of global brands can crowd out and erode local products, traditions and identity.
**Tax avoidance:** transfer pricing and complex structures can shift reported profit to low-tax jurisdictions, reducing the tax actually paid in the host country.

sections.content: In Paper 1 you are given a case study, so never answer a 'host country impact' question with a generic list. Hunt the stimulus for specific evidence - the number of jobs, the country's level of development, whether the MNC exports or sells locally, any mention of wages or environmental concern - and tie each positive and negative to that evidence. A balanced argument anchored in the case study, ending in a judgement, is what reaches the top band.

sections.question: Global Motors, an MNC, opens a car factory in the developing nation of Equatoria, creating 1,200 jobs. Increased competition and automation then force three local car-part suppliers to close, causing 250 job losses. The average annual wage at Global Motors is $9,000; at the closed local firms it was $7,000. Calculate the net impact on employment and on total annual wages in the local community. [4]
sections.solution:
This question rewards calculation followed by a short quantitative interpretation.

**Step 1: Net change in employment.**
1,200 jobs created − 250 jobs lost = **950 net new jobs** (a positive employment effect).

**Step 2: Total new wages created by the MNC.**
1,200 × $9,000 = $10,800,000

**Step 3: Total wages lost from the closed local firms.**
250 × $7,000 = $1,750,000

**Step 4: Net change in total annual wages.**
$10,800,000 − $1,750,000 = **$9,050,000** injected into the local economy each year.

**Interpretation:** in purely quantitative terms the net impact is strongly positive - 950 more jobs and over $9 million more in annual wages, with Global Motors also paying a higher average wage than the firms that closed. The closures are a genuine cost, but on these figures the MNC raises both employment and total local income.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **AO2 (1):** correct net employment figure (950).
- **AO2 (1):** correct MNC wage total ($10.8m).
- **AO2 (1):** correct lost-wage total ($1.75m) and net wage change ($9.05m).
- **AO4 (1):** a clear interpretation that the net effect on jobs and wages is positive.

Remember the calculation captures only the measurable side. A fuller 'discuss' question would also weigh profit repatriation, working conditions and the harm to the displaced suppliers - numbers alone do not settle whether Equatoria is better off.

items: **Calling an exporter an MNC** - selling products abroad is exporting; a business is only multinational once it owns and runs operations in another country (foreign direct investment). Getting this definition wrong undermines the whole answer.

**Giving a one-sided answer** - writing only benefits ('MNCs create jobs and investment') or only harms ('MNCs just exploit workers') caps the answer in the lower bands. 'Discuss' and 'evaluate' demand both sides.

**Listing impacts without applying them** - a generic list of advantages and disadvantages earns AO1 only. The marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business and host country in the case study.

**Confusing profit repatriation with tax avoidance** - repatriation is sending profit back to the home country; tax avoidance (via transfer pricing) is shifting reported profit to low-tax jurisdictions. They are related but distinct negatives - use the right term.

**Ignoring the host country's context** - the same MNC can help a high-unemployment developing country far more than a wealthy one. Failing to use the country's level of development wastes easy application marks.
**Assuming all MNCs behave identically** - some exploit and pollute, others invest responsibly (CSR). Judge the specific business in the stimulus rather than asserting that all MNCs are good or bad.
**Reaching no supported judgement** - a 'discuss' or 'evaluate' answer that gives both sides but never decides which outweighs, and why, cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer - marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 1.6 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business and country in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'discuss' and 'evaluate' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion - never to a generic list.

sections.question: Discuss the impact of a multinational company setting up operations in a developing host country. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
When a multinational company (MNC) makes a foreign direct investment in a developing host country - say, building a large electronics factory - the most immediate impact is on employment and income. In a developing country where unemployment and underemployment are typically high, an MNC that hires thousands of local workers directly reduces joblessness, and the wages it pays are then spent in the local economy, supporting shops, landlords and suppliers. Because the MNC also brings capital, modern equipment and management expertise, it can raise the host country's GDP and transfer technology and skills that local workers keep even if they later move to domestic firms. Alongside this, the government gains tax revenue from profits, wages and sales, which it can spend on public services, and consumers gain wider choice. For a developing economy short of capital and expertise, these are substantial benefits.

However, the impact is far from wholly positive. Much of the profit the factory generates may be repatriated - sent back to the MNC's home-country headquarters rather than reinvested in the host economy - so the country keeps the jobs but loses a large share of the wealth. The MNC's economies of scale and pricing power can also drive smaller local manufacturers out of business, causing offsetting job losses and weakening domestic industry. In a developing country with limited regulatory capacity, there is a real risk of exploitation - low wages, long hours or unsafe conditions - and of environmental damage if the MNC cuts costs by polluting or depleting resources. The dominance of a global brand can also erode local products and culture, and tax avoidance through transfer pricing may mean the promised tax revenue never fully materialises.

The balance between these effects depends heavily on the specifics. If the MNC pays fair wages, trains local workers, buys from local suppliers and reinvests a meaningful share of profit, the net impact on a capital-poor developing country is likely to be strongly positive - the jobs, investment and technology transfer address exactly the constraints such economies face. If instead it repatriates nearly all profit, undercuts local firms, exploits weak regulation and pollutes, the host country may gain some employment but bear heavy long-term costs.

**Judgement.** On balance, for most developing host countries the impact of an MNC setting up operations is net positive, but conditional. The decisive factor is that developing economies are chronically short of the very things an MNC brings - capital, jobs, technology and tax revenue - so even after allowing for profit repatriation and pressure on local firms, the inflow of investment and skills usually leaves the country better off than the alternative of no FDI at all. That judgement holds only where the host government can regulate wages, conditions and the environment effectively; where it cannot, the negatives of exploitation and environmental harm can outweigh the gains. The single most important step, therefore, is a host government that attaches conditions to the investment so the benefits are captured and the harms contained.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 - knowledge (MNC concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on - what an MNC and FDI are, and the specific impacts (jobs, investment, technology transfer, tax revenue, choice; profit repatriation, competition for local firms, exploitation, environmental and cultural effects). Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 - application to the developing host country:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to THIS context - high pre-existing unemployment, shortage of capital and skills, and weak regulatory capacity that raises the risk of exploitation and pollution. Generic points that could describe any country do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 - balanced analysis and supported judgement:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning - benefits (jobs, investment, technology, tax) weighed against costs (repatriation, local competition, exploitation, environment) - and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to a conclusion and justifies WHY one side outweighs the other for a developing host country, including the condition (effective government regulation) on which the verdict depends.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus a conditional, justified judgement. The engine rewards an answer that applies the impacts to a developing economy and decides; it does NOT reward a longer generic list of pros and cons with no context and no verdict.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-positive or all-negative answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct impacts with no link to the developing-country context.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way - AO1 for the right MNC concepts, AO2 for applying them to the specific business and host country in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list impacts: name the concept (FDI, technology transfer, profit repatriation), apply it to the country in the case, weigh positives against negatives, and finish by committing to a judgement with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.

MNCs connect directly to the rest of the course. The reasons firms go multinational feed into growth and evolution, where you will study methods of expansion such as mergers, acquisitions and joint ventures. The host-country impacts link to stakeholders and to corporate social responsibility, where MNC behaviour is judged against ethical and environmental expectations. And the two-sided, apply-then-judge habit built here - identify the concept, apply it to the specific business and country, weigh both sides, then commit to a supported judgement - is the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.
